comments.title: Wounds
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Several times now, I've come across the concept of wounds as doorways in pop culture. I wonder whether there's actually some truth to that and not just a fanciful fabrication.

Obviously, on a material level, wounds serve as gateways for germs and dirt into the bloodstream. Some initiatory rituals involve (intentional) wounding and we might say that the initiation is delivered through the wounds—though that might well be a stretch.

I'd also draw a connection to stigmata. According to Thomas of Celano's First and Second Lives of Saint Francis, When [Saint Francis received a vision of a crucified angel] he was filled with wonder, but he did not know what the vision meant. [...] He wondered anxiously what this vision could mean, and his soul was uneasy as it searched for understanding. And as his understanding sought in vain for an explanation and his heart was filled with perplexity at the great novelty of this vision, the marks of nails began to appear in his hands and feet, just as he had seen them slightly earlier in the crucified man above him. Reading this with my above musings in mind, the word filled stands out—did the wonder and perplexity enter through the wounds? Or, to be more precise, might his stigmata be the material representation of the entryway through which these emotions filled him?

This matter interests me not just as theoretical speculation but also because of the practical implications. Suppose one was to cut oneself in an act of self-harm¹. Might demons and other spiritual nasties consider this an invitation? Attracting these things certainly seems to fit with the intention of hurting oneself.

—David P.

1: Considering only those acts meant to cause needless harm. Drawing blood for medical or magical reasons, the mentioned initiatory rituals, etc. don't count.

replies.title: Re: Wounds
replies.content:
Some wounds might be a gateway to past hurts that can be healed...

Interestingly, from my current reading, occultist Aidan Wachter quotes what some spirits he works with told him about wounds in his book "Weaving Fate: Hypersigils, Changing the Past & Telling True Lies":

"There are traumas in living. Damages. Wounds. Some heal like they never were. These fall away and do no harm. Some like there was a fragment of broken spear point left in the wound, and so though the wound has closed, it festers, brings fevers and illness, and sometimes death. These are the wounds in which the seeds of suffering are sown.

When these types of harm occur, by the malice of men or angry spirits, by trespass or accident, by pure chance or a wrong step during the hunt, they fester inside us like the spear-wound. But these are soul wounds and this infection we call the Fever Stone.

The Fever Stone is neutral and bears no malice; it is an effect rather than a cause. The stone creates a point of illness in the spirit or soul and it dwells inside the body until they die, and sometimes even beyond death until something dislodges the Stone.

Sometimes the Fever Stone is so strong that it attaches to the line of Ancestors that stand behind the person so that the stone that festers inside the person is an old Stone, whose harm has passed down the ages. Most have a stone like this, sometimes nearly benign, sometimes malign. First we must heal what we can of the damage these ancestral stones have caused. Then we can move onto the stones that rest inside the individual."

He then gives a practice called washing the stone to heal this wound, among other techniques. I haven't worked with his techniques myself, but I am intrigued by them, and doing some other reading until I begin my next course of study around the winter solstice. I might work with some of this material.

No real question this week, but two observations to share:

a) I had a very odd thought about the No Kings Protest. The most famous American King, to my mind, is Martin Luther King. I'm thus rather troubled by the thought that by pushing the "No Kings" symbolism, this creates what is basically a force pushing to "cancel" Martin Luther King. While there's unlikely to be much magical influence here given the lack of anything that focuses power, it is a startlingly good example of why it is so important to carefully pick symbolism.

b) A little while back, I asked about a reading for a potential romantic partner. The reading had as the witnesses amissio and carcer, and Fortuna Major for the judge. We discussed the reading, you said it seemed to be saying it was possible, but there would be delays and difficulties.

I ran into some difficulties which seemed like they would be pretty serious issues, and gave it some more thought: I had asked if a relationship was possible, not if it was a good idea. I followed up with asking if I would be happy: the result was laetetia for the right witness, tristia for the left, and carcer for the judge.

This seems to be saying I would be good while it lasted, but things would not work out well in the end. All in all, it looks like the oracle agrees with my sense that this would be a bad idea; and perhaps the amissio in the initial reading was saying I'd lose more than just my heart if I went down this road.

I'm sharing this because I've seen warnings to be very careful about exactly how you phrase questions of an oracle; and crucially, asking if something is possible is rarely the only question worth asking...

comments.title: Compassion/Love
comments.content:
Theres many modern traditions and teachers that emphasize love or compassion as a central theme with the divine. From the teachings of Jesus, to Chenrezig/Kwan Yin in the East. "God is love" seems to be a central theme.

I've noticed this is somewhat absent or not a big focus of occult traditions. Where does compassion/love fit into Occult traditions?

Thanks for your time,
Mauve Senescent Seahorse

replies.title: Re: Compassion/Love
replies.content:
The theme of compassion or unconditional love is a central theme only in certain prophetic religions in the Piscean era -- roughly speaking, in the last 2000 years or so. Even there it's far from universal -- Avalokiteshvara (aka Chenrezig, Kuan Yin, etc.) is only one bodhisattva out of many, you know. It's become a fad recently to redefine all religious and spiritual paths as based on compassion, but like most such redefinitions, that one requires a lot of handwaving and some actual falsification.

Occultism is not a religion and it's not meant to replace religion. Historically, most Western occultists have been active in religious traditions outside of occultism -- Christianity, Judaism, some form of Paganism, or what have you. In its proper role occultism doesn't try to replace religion or fill religious needs, and so compassion isn't that relevant to the tradition -- however relevant it may be to individual occultists.

replies.title: Re: Compassion/Love
replies.content:
"In its proper role occultism doesn't try to replace religion or fill religious needs, and so compassion isn't that relevant to the tradition"

I've always thought of compassion as being an ability to encompass more than one perspective and to gain understanding from seeing a wider view. An attribute that religion may adopt (or not) but in fact a tool completely independent of any particular religion.
A way to 'see' and go beyond viewing only via 'self'
Also that compassion is another of those words that has been roughly mangled and often misunderstood. Anyone conflating compassion with whatever they mean by 'unconditional love' would do well to consider your excellent aphorism [iirc]:
"Sometimes the best way to demonstrate unconditional love is with the blade of a sword"

From an occult perspective, I've always liked the idea of compassion as a tool of balance... something along the lines of 'wisdom without compassion risks being cruelty'

Wasn't there someone who defined compassion as being creative enough to know how to jolt someone from stupor to sensibility - no lovey-dovey kindness and empathy necessarily required. 'Be compassionate' or its lack lustre relative 'be kind' have so often been twisted where the desired meaning turns out to be 'do what I want or you are a bad person'... cue edge of sword.

[earthworm]

The mob intend to destroy the village. The villager has much love of the village and villagers and compassion revealed that the mob love and believe that killing everyone in the village is 'a good thing' and that they believe they are being compassionate and loving by intending to kill everyone quickly - especially the children, because that is the compassionate way to do things.
By understanding these things, a choice can be better made - and so, taking up a sword, the villager holds fast at the gates and the mob loses enthusiasm as a compassionate sword wielded with love makes an ocean of blood.
The village is saved; unconditional love of the villagers and village and the compassion of understanding expressed with a blade required a sacrifice; and the sacrifice was that the villager's body dies of wounds received.

Bit on the fly and don't know whether that works or not.

[earthworm]

You need to learn to be conscious of his influence while it's happening. Once you can do that -- and it may take quite a bit of practice! -- you can choose whether to be affected by it or not.

replies.content: I've also had good results with a tealight diffuser (where you put a few drops of an appropriate essential oil in a little bowl of water over a tealight candle). If you use a beeswax tealight especially, I find it much less offensive to the lungs than incense smoke, and you can inscribe/dress the candle and the little aluminum cup. I use that for my altar. If it's more about cleansing a space/smoking a room or object, I will substitute a room spray for the incense: a little alcohol, some water, and some essential oils in a spray bottle. You can get fancier and tincture herbs/resins in the alcohol and/or use a hydrosol or floral water such as rose or orange blossom water instead of plain water, and even add appropriate semiprecious stones to the spray bottle. I also like the potpourri method for my altar. All three methods (diffuser, spray, potpourri) seem to work for me in applications where the goal is mostly to affect my own consciousness through scent.

comments.title: Enneagram and Jhana references
comments.content: I was the anon who asked about the Enneagram a couple weeks ago. I want to thank the anon who pointed me towards the Gurdjieff biography, the harmonious circle. It appears a good as place to start as any in my inquiry into its roots! I also plan to try correlating the 9 types of the psychological Enneagram with numerology. Perhaps starting with assuming people (including myself) are acting according to a particular Enneagram type, on the day their personal time number corresponds to that number, and seeing how well that appears to explain the situation and dynamics. Paying it forward, another asked about Jhana. I'd recommend the work of Daniel Ingram, particularly his book, Mastering the Core Teachings of the Buddha, (2nd edition), freely available online. Particularly sections relating Jhana to powers. His observation is that the powerful concentration resulting from Jhanic states makes magical phenomenon almost inevitable / unavoidable. As to whether they are revealed or constructed... I won't dispute the assertion they are constructed, but they do appear constructed in a way that is closely aligned with natural properties of attention and development. For example, the progress of insight can be mapped onto the progression of jhanas. So can predictable sequences in spiritual dreams journeys. Vince Horn, also in the pragmatic dharma lineage, has talked about how the progression of jhanas follows the principle of pressure = force / area. Initially, high concentration wants to be focused on a single point (1st and 2nd Jhana). As the force of concentration increases, it has a natural tendency to be dispersed into ever wider perceptual areas (3rd to 8th Jhana, increasing in subtlety). The connections between Jhana and magic are something I hope to explore more as I progress in both practices.

comments.title: No Kings: The Resistance
comments.content:
This is not a question but a further comment on the No Kings protests' atrocious symbolism. Apparently, they have adopted Star Wars imagery: https://media1-production-mightynetworks.imgix.net/asset/f95dd498-2409-451e-87a0-7155fe2e44b8/image000000_2_.png

See the orange logos? That's the symbol of the Resistance. For those fortunate enough to not have seen the Disney trilogy, the Resistance is basically the Rebel Alliance twenty years or so after the Rebels won.

Why is this bad symbolism? Well, for a start, the Rebels won and created the New Republic. For some reasons that the movies never bothered to properly explain, they somehow missed a new empire raising a giant army and building a third Death Star. Thus, they became the underdog once again. While this may serve the interests of the movies (assuming one believes just repeating the original trilogy with different characters is what makes good movies), it's not really a position you want to be placed into, is it?

Worse, the Resistance is utterly incapable, existing only to be saved by Rey and a select few other characters. Most of them are wiped out at various points in the movies. Whereas the Rebel Alliance of old was a proper small army, at some point the entire Resistance fits into the Millenium Falcon. Again, not really something I personally would want to manifest.

I'm also wondering whether the Resistance might not have put itself into this position by choice. As I said, the movies don't explain their backstory particularly well, but in Episode VII, the third Death Star is used to destroy the New Republic's core worlds, implying that the New Republic is still around. And yet, the Resistance is already its own organisation. Just what are they resisting? The empire (I mean First Order) or Law and Order in general?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: No Kings: The Resistance
replies.content:
This is good. I'd also point out that the entire concept of "the Resistance" means that they've abandoned any positive goals; all they do is resist Trump, or more precisely, the vast projection they've placed on a cheesy reality TV star turned politician. That guarantees their defeat, since -- as I noted in a blog post -- most Americans were deeply and reasonably dissatisfied with the pre-Trump status quo, and if your slogan amounts to "Vote for us so things will continue to suck!" you won't get far.

But the Star Wars franchise displays this in an even more overblown form, as you've shown. The core gimmick of the franchise is that the Empire cannot be defeated, just dealt a sequence of temporary setbacks, and the Rebels cannot win, they can just delay the inevitable by some combination of virtue signaling and improbable heroics.

Among other things, this means that the franchise can never move beyond the basic framing of the first film, which it endlessly rehashes in different forms -- and that, in turn, suggests that the people manufacturing the franchise can't let themselves think about what would happen if the supposed good guys won once and for all. That might be worth a parody one of these days.

replies.title: Re: No Kings: The Resistance
replies.content:
Close to being parody of the franchise...

I've noticed that there has been a growing trend among some dissident right wing vloggers and podcasters where they invert the overall narrative and posit a thought experiment along the lines of, "What if the Empire are the good guys and that what we see in the films is just Rebel Alliance propaganda?" This has led to some rather hilarious revisionisms, like the Sith actually being something akin to Stoic sages who balance both sides of the Force, and the Jedi are like religious fanatics who lean in only to one side and end up embodying a very unbalanced use of it, ect. The more serious of this commentary advances the idea that the primary motive of the franchise was to create a postwar liberal morality play.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I asked a question last time (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/345116.html?thread=57676316#cmt57676316) about determining the identity of a spirit that may have been unhappy with me via geomancy.

I conducted a series of readings to narrow down the identity from some possibilities, and the answer seems to be a being related to a prior religious commitment, as I had suggested.

The story here is that I was a fairly devout soft polytheist at one point. Due to my theology at the time, I strung together lists of names of deities with vaguely similar aspects. When my theology changed to being a hard polytheist, I figured I should probably approach individuals as individuals and stop doing the list thing, but that left me with the problem of figuring out who exactly I should focus on from each list. I didn't do divination, but instead followed the advice that it's best to start by focusing on a single pantheon, and so did an interpretatio graeca kind of thing (I had joined ADF at that time, and that was the culture they included that seemed to be the best fit for the largest number of beings I have previously attempted to interact with.). My hypothesis now is that someone was left out of that equation.

The reason I decided to ask about a spirit influencing the situation was because an area of my life (which is in fact the area of life the being in question is associated with) was unusually fortunate while I was a soft polytheist, but became gradually more unfortunate after I made the change described above, and has been resistant to other mundane and magical attempts at amelioration, while divination frustratingly insisted that the mundane and magical things I was trying were in fact all working just fine despite the lack of results.

1) Broadly, do you have any advice for navigating this situation?

2) I'm thinking of various possible geomancy questions to ask about possible options for improving this situation, but finding it hard to narrow down exactly what to ask. Do you have any suggestions?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Geomancy's a very challenging resource to use for this sort of question; it's not the resource I would use, for example. Have you considered returning to your former theology and seeing if that ameliorates matters? Alternatively, have you considered going to someone who specializes in that pantheon and asking for help?

comments.title: Astrology as Divination
comments.content: I've been thinking about the discussion in the comments the other week about how astrology is an empirical science. I decided to finally stick my nose in Geoffrey Cornelius's 'The Moment of Astrology' and he seems to be making a strong argument that trying to prove astrology by "scientific" means is a losing game for astrologers, and that it's better to see astrology as originating in divination. He says that many of the studies done for astrologers have turned out very bad, such as the Suicide Study. Have you read this book/what do you think?

replies.title: Re: Astrology as Divination
replies.content: I haven't read it, no. I agree that trying to prove astrology to the satisfaction of scientific materialists is a chump's game, but that's for reasons John McClenon explains in his book Deviant Science: The Case of Parapsychology. Since it doesn't matter to me two weak farts in a Cat 5 hurricane whether scientific materialists believe in astrology or not, I consider the proof that matters to be my own success in making predictions and electing times for actions.

replies.title: Re: Astrology as Divination
replies.content: Well worth the read. He was a master of the art, a deep thinker, a good writer, and this seems to be his Magnum Opus.

comments.title: Muses - Good and Bad
comments.content:
Hi John,

I hope you’re well. I’m a mystical/magical type and creative writer. I’m working on a new story in the fantasy fiction genre. My subconscious seems to be very alive with this story and is feeding me very concrete ideas about the world and characters, which I generally find helpful. I do have a friend who is both a mystic and a successful published writer that wrote a trilogy that she claims some darker questionable spiritual beings inspired in her and the story created a problematic bond with these beings for her. I’m much more familiar with the idea of muses being positive influences, and I’m definitely open to this sort of experience. In fact, I can’t even imagine getting through a novel without a muse-like spiritual ally. I wonder if you could speak to how I might work in such a way that doesn’t block positive help and support and ways I might limit any problematic/parasitic influences in my creative writing if you believe such a thing exists. My story pertains to the fae which I have a deep Scottish/Welsh ancestral connection with so I could see some of that energy coming through the work. Thanks for any insight you have on the writing process for mystical/channeling types.

Sending love your way.

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content:
The kind of spiritual influence you attract is an exact reflection of your own spiritual state, so your best option if you want to attract positive spiritual influences is to clean up your own act. Regular practice of religious, spiritual, or positive occult exercises is a good way to do this -- and of course it helps if you make an effort to be a decent person.

This sort of writing is something I've experienced, btw -- I had the first volume of my fantasy series The Weird of Hali download itself into my brain over a few days, and I wrote the whole thing in a rush -- it took me only eight weeks to complete a 70,000-word fantasy novel. That said, I don't recommend letting yourself get too dependent on the help of spirits. You're perfectly capable of writing a novel yourself -- the late Marion Zimmer Bradley used to say that anyone who could write a literate English sentence could make a living in genre fiction. Put some effort into developing your own strength of will and self-confidence, and you'll be fine.

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content:
"The kind of spiritual influence you attract is an exact reflection of your own spiritual state"

Do most of the sprits that guide or influence typical humans tend to be at a similar stage to us in their evolution, i.e. etherically or astrally based but working on developing their mental bodies?

Patrick

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content: No, not at all. The issue is that your spiritual state determines the plane on which your mind operates -- which can be anything from the bottom end of the astral, which is hardly distinguishable from mindless animal passion, to the top end, which is infused with the mental plane and thus full of meaning, purpose, and value. It's as though your mind is a radio -- and you decide, by your spiritual state, what station to tune in to.

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content:
So do we attract spirits whose minds can operate in the same subplane as ours, regardless of their level of development?

Patrick

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content: We attract spirits who inhabit the level on which our minds operate.

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content: So we attract spirits whose densest parts are of the subplane in which our minds operate?

replies.title: Re: Muses - Good and Bad
replies.content: That I can't tell you. We attract spirits who operate on the level on which our minds operate.

comments.title: discovery one's life purpose
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

There's this (New Age?) idea that we are all here on earth for some specific purpose and that somehow, whether it be through meditation, divine intervention or some other life changing experience, a person can have the knowledge of that purpose bestowed upon him and then dedicate his life to it.

Contrary to this, I seem to recall you stating that it's up to us as individual's to actively choose our direction / purpose in life, rather than waiting passively for it to show up and guide us. I can see how meditation helps us choose a direction wisely as it helps us think clearly, but still a choice is made.

What does occultism have to say about all this re: life purpose?

Thank you,
Tim PW

replies.title: Re: discovery one's life purpose
replies.content: I think some people come into life with a specific purpose -- a mission to carry out, some particular karmic burden to work off, or what have you. Many, many others do not. Most often, we're in incarnation for the purpose of experiencing and learning from incarnation, and how we choose to do that is up to us.

comments.title: Post on How to Learn and Question on Getting through Tough Times
comments.content:
Good Afternoon,

May the new moon bring everyone a fresh start wherever you most need it.

To Share: I'm continuing my introductory series over at Rhetoric for the Renaissance Man, this time laying out some basics on "how to learn," the idea being that Renaissance Men need to be broadly knowledgeable, so it would be helpful to have some guidance on how to get that way: https://rhetoricfortherenaissanceman.com/2025/10/15/learning-the-materials-from-which-we-build-everything-else/

To Ask: The past 1.5 - 2 years have been something of a challenge (to put it mildly). I've mostly consistently stuck with my spiritual work throughout, which I think is a help, and whenever I pray or do divination on how to get past the challenges, I get an answer something like "stick with the work." Being rather fiery and airy by nature, this kind of "just let things happen" is hard for me to accept, but I'm doing my best. Besides "grit your teeth and keep up the daily practices," any advice on how to get through such periods of things not going as I might hope in the near to medium term?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Post on How to Learn and Question on Getting through Tough Times
replies.content: I wish I had any more helpful advice, but Saturn's strong in my chart and so the only thing that's ever worked for me is just trudging onward.

replies.title: Re: Post on How to Learn and Question on Getting through Tough Times
replies.content:
Thanks for sharing our Renaissance Man posts.

For your ask, this may be related or of some help but today, ran across a new Rune Soup Youtube post by Gordon White in which he gives a list of 10 Magic Habits.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1eCWDrNRUJ8

Of interest, the first habit mentioned relates to "praying morning and night" and that habit or thought is "It's OK to suck, it's not OK to skip". replies.title: Re: Post on How to Learn and Question on Getting through Tough Times
replies.content:
Thanks very much for this, I will check it out, and I agree with JMG, that's great advice, pithier and clearer than my usual way of putting it: "consistency is more important than intensity."

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Post on How to Learn and Question on Getting through Tough Times
replies.content:
Jeff,

Just my two cents… In general, when I find myself slogging along at a practice, I think of that as a warning sign. It represents lack of enthusiasm (call it passion, drive, whatever), and to me Job One is finding a way to rekindle that enthusiasm.

That could be reading something that sheds a different light on the material (Dion Fortune’s ‘circuit of force’ provided me a lot of insight into the Dolmen Arch course). It could be trying something different, experimentation (like getting more resonance when vibrating the divine names).

Just examples, the point being that maintaining interest and passion is like the difference between sailing with the wind instead of against. Not only is it more fun, but in my experience the results are better.

replies.title: Re: Post on How to Learn and Question on Getting through Tough Times
replies.content:

The advice to try out some different things and see if something new sparks it, though (whether magical or not) is likely very helpful.

Thanks again,
Jeff

comments.title: Cheese Magic by Erika Kubick
comments.content:
Someone here was asking about food magic last week. Here is a book about cheese magic. It is a bit cheesy, but the dairy oriented pagan foodies here might like it. It's kind of magic lite really. Just a seasonal food guide for the 8 spoked wheel of the year.

"a book of 100 seasonal and mouthwatering recipes, plates, and pairings alongside gorgeous photography—all inspired by the festivals of the pagan Wheel of the Year.

Discover delectable enchantment and delicious flavors with the latest exceptional cheese book from visionary cheesemonger Erika Kubick, with more than 100 seasonal cheese recipes, plates, and pairings inspired by the pagan calendar. Captured in rich, eye-popping photography and accompanied by wickedly beautiful illustrations by Devin Forst, cheese becomes an edible manifestation of seasonal celebration.

Divided into eight sections, this gorgeous compendium begins with the winter solstice and a brûléed feta fruit salad. It then carries you to the spring equinox with goat cheese scones. Welcome summer with the full moon plate and spicy honey. And finish the journey on Samhaim, the Witches' New Year, with a mushroom and brie tart."

In other words there is a market for this kind of thing, if someone wanted to go further... well, they could.

replies.title: Re: Cheese Magic by Erika Kubick
replies.content: I'm trying, without much luck, to avoid asking if she's a big cheese in the Pagan scene...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I would like to ask about a quote from your Wednesday post:

"The school of occultism that I practice and teach holds that consciousness isn’t just a passive blank that receives impressions. Here again, meditation is the key. With practice, it becomes possible to perceive structures within individual consciousness. These structures have an active dimension, which we can call will or intentionality, that balances the receptive dimension of consciousness as such. These structures make up what this school of thought calls the individuality or higher self, which is distinct from the personality or lower self."

I recall something you said years ago (?) that consciousness was a grade of will, and also that consciousness arises out of the will meeting an obstacle. It sounds in the quote above that Will is a part of consciousness, which would reverse that relationship. It's probable that things have gotten a bit garbled in my mind, and I hope you don't mind clarifying this for me.

You also said at some point that the spark that was sent out by the source had Being, Will and Consciousness as it's makeup. This aligns with what I have come to believe (I could be wrong) that the source of all things is

Stillness, which is Being, just pure Eternal Being.
Movement, which is Will, though not focused on anything yet. Just eternal pure Movement, which is paradoxically happening at the same time as Stillness.
Awareness, which is Consciousness, and which then gives a focus to Movement, and a direction to Will.

I don't think this contradicts anything you have said but it leaves a confused impression in my mind as I try to sort this out. I can't say I have gone exploring the structures of consciousness yet, and if I have I am not aware of it, so maybe that's the problem right there. Or maybe my model is wrong. Or maybe we are saying the same thing but using different ways of saying it.

Would you mind pointing me in the right direction? I won't be upset if you say I should just keep meditating.

Thank you as always for your patience!

-Myriam

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There is no right direction. There are different collections of primate hoots and grunts -- we call those "words" to be polite -- that more or less express the way somebody or other experiences the world. If your model of being, will, and consciousness works for you, then by all means use it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hmm! Yes, I should have remembered that about words. Thank you.

It does work, especially fitting in well with the rest of what I am doing. As you have mentioned before, if it works, it's valid. That too I should have remembered.

comments.title: Questions about helping others
comments.content:
Hello JMG and everyone!

About three weeks ago, I performed my ritual initiation of the GSF and I am currently practicing with the initiate's exercises and SOP.

1) First, I would like to know if there is any issue or interference with having my cats in the same room when I open the lodge. The first time it happened, I only realized after I had sit down and started working that one of them was inside the boundaries of the lodge. I didn't notice anything unusual, and throughout the session, the cat remained still and asleep in the same spot. In the next ceremonies I assured that they where not in the boundaries, but they remained in the room.

2) I have received a request for blood donation for an acquaintance who is scheduled for surgery. I used to donate regularly, but now I wonder if doing so could interfere with my current practices or trigger any unwanted situations.

3) Finally, with her permission, I would like to consult you about an experience a friend had:

She has a very strong connection with her mother, despite their spiritual practices and religions being different. Last week, while riding the subway, she suddenly felt intense heat, agitation, cold sweat and then experienced a ringing in her right ear along with the sensation of a liquid dripping from it. When she checked herself, there was nothing. Some time later, upon arriving home, she found out that her mother had fallen down the stairs and injured her head, right above her right ear. Fortunately, it wasn't serious, and she is now recovering with some stitches.

My friend has asked for help to understand what might have happened, if there is any literature that discusses this type of phenomenon, and what advice you might offer for handling it. She also mentioned having had similar experiences with everyday but no so serious situations, like feeling that same sensation of anxiety and later finding out her son forgot his homework at home and received a poor grade.

Thank you!

Jimena

comments.title: Reiki
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I learned Reiki back in the late ‘80s before it became super popular and everyone was calling himself a Reiki Master.

I recently came across a Shinto healer who is part Japanese named Benton Ryer. He says that Reiki is not what it seems. He says the term reiki refers to ghosts and other spirits that aren’t elevated. He has a video about this here:

https://youtu.be/yu5Yuzv7bk0?si=-EHB7ObcOvyu56W9

He’s also pointed out in interviews that Usui died at age 60 and that his successor Hayashi died by suicide. He says that Reiki practitioners typically see an improvement in health and energy at first, but they gradually decline because Reiki is connected with parasitic entities. And most people never learn the truth about Reiki because they don’t know Japanese language or folklore.

I know you have some experience with Shinto and Japanese traditions. Do you know if there’s any validity in what he’s saying?

replies.title: Re: Reiki
replies.content:
First, the Japanese language has a lot of homophones -- words that sound the same but have different meanings. Reiki written with the usual characters, 霊気, means "spiritual or universal life force" -- but there will be many different characters pronounced "rei" and "ki," and Ryer will likely have another of those in mind.

Second, I don't know enough about reiki to judge whether he's accurate in his broader thesis. It's not something that ever really interested me. It's not too hard to get information in English these days about Japanese folklore, so you might see what you can find.

replies.title: Re: Free online horoscopes
replies.content: The value of free online horoscopes is purely that they'll cast the chart for you, and generally get it very precisely correct. The interpretations are all garbage, and there's no way they can be anything else, because every factor in a horoscope must be interpreted in the light of all the others. The thing to do once you have your chart is to get some books -- Julia and Derek Parker's Parkers Astrology is a good first choice -- and begin exploring your horoscope from the perspectives of multiple writers.

comments.title: Origins of Kabbalah
comments.content:
Good evening JMG,

I recall reading your potted summary of the history of the Kabbalah at some point. I vaguely recall that you described it's pre-Jewish origins. Now everything a certain major search engine pulls up says it was Jewish. Am I miss remembering?

I'm sure every group will try to make it their own and take credit. As I'm learning it from a Jewish perspective, I was wondering if, in fact, the rabbinic writers might have come across some proto-Kabbalah and then given it a Jewish flavor to make it palatable to other Jews vs in fact being the originators. Or, maybe, the better question, how great is their contribution to the body of Kabbalah (vs other sources)?

I'm sure it's a subject for a book, but I'm just curious what your "off the top of your head" thoughts are here.

replies.title: Re: Origins of Kabbalah
replies.content:
Gershom Scholem, still the best historian of the Cabala (and a Jew), showed in his book The Origins of the Kabbalah that it wasn't originally Jewish at all, but was borrowed into Judaism from the Gnostic movement in the 11th century AD. As for search engines, I don't recommend trusting those!

The early Jewish Cabalists took the tradition from the Gnostics and reworked it to fit the needs of their religion. By the 14th century the same tradition had been taken up, partly via Jewish sources, by Christian Cabalists, and that latter movement is what gave rise to the modern occult Cabala. It's borrowed considerably from the Jewish Cabala from time to time, but its core is Middle Platonism with a strong Pythagorean influence.
